title: Spatial overhead reduction for 2D hypergraph product codes
authors: Aarav Pabla, Yu-Xin Wang, Yifan Hong
abstract: The hypergraph product creates a quantum stabilizer code from two input classical linear codes; a paradigmatic example being the surface code as a hypergraph product of two classical repetition codes. Many properties of the hypergraph product code can be inherited from those of the classical codes such as the code dimension, minimum distance and certain fault-tolerant gadgets. We investigate ways to reduce the number of physical qubits in hypergraph product codes while maintaining some of their useful properties for fault tolerance. We show that the code dimension, canonical logical basis, and minimum distances of the hypergraph product code are preserved through this reduction. We also provide distance-preserving syndrome measurement schedules as well as examples of reduced hypergraph product codes with parameter improvements such as $[\![610,64,6]\!] \rightarrow [\![441,64,6]\!]$ and $[\![1225,49,11]\!] \rightarrow [\![931,49,11]\!]$. In memory simulations with circuit-level depolarizing noise, we observe that the reduced codes can have similar subthreshold performance as their unreduced versions, but using fewer physical qubits. Finally, we show how overhead reduction can be compatible with homomorphic measurement gadgets, fold-transversal gates and automorphisms, which extends the savings to logical computation.
summary: This paper proposes a method to significantly reduce the number of physical qubits needed for 2D hypergraph product codes without losing error-correcting distance or logical dimension. By strategically combining stabilizers, the authors achieve much higher code rates and demonstrate that these reduced codes remain effective for fault-tolerant quantum computing.
main_problem: The high physical qubit overhead required for 2D hypergraph product (HGP) codes is a barrier to practical implementation. The paper investigates whether the number of physical qubits can be reduced without sacrificing code distance, logical dimension, or fault-tolerant capabilities.
main_result: The authors develop a method to reduce spatial overhead by removing check-type qubits while preserving the code dimension, minimum distance, and compatibility with fault-tolerant gadgets. They demonstrate significant parameter improvements, such as reducing a [[610, 64, 6]] code to a [[441, 64, 6]] code, with comparable subthreshold performance under circuit-level noise.
method: The reduction procedure involves combining stabilizers and removing check-type qubits using a coloring-based partitioning of the check-adjacency graph. The optimal reduction schedule is determined by mapping the problem to a maximum-weight matching problem solvable via the Hungarian algorithm.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 2D Hypergraph Product (HGP) codes, a class of quantum stabilizer (CSS) codes constructed from two classical linear codes. The noise model used for performance benchmarking is circuit-level depolarizing noise.
key_observables: Code dimension (k), minimum distance (dX, dZ), qubit weight (wq), check weight (wc), and error thresholds (subthreshold performance).
important_parameters: Code rate (k/n), relative distance, and the weights of the reduced parity-check matrices.
main_assumptions: The reduction assumes the hardware supports the long-range connectivity required by the underlying qLDPC structure and that all logical qubits reside in a single HGP sector.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate Tanner graphs for classical and quantum codes, the general structure of HGP codes, the bipartite graph construction for the reduction algorithm, and examples of optimal vs. non-optimal reduction schedules.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spatial overhead, presents the qubit reduction procedure and its mathematical proofs for property preservation, details the algorithmic approach using graph theory for optimal scheduling, provides numerical simulations for noise performance, and concludes with the compatibility of reduced codes with fault-tolerant computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. General structure of the hypergraph product. The top-left section of qubits are the bit-type qubits, and the bottom-right section of qubits are the check-type qubits. Some edges are explicitly shown; the rest of the edges (not shown in the figure) inherit the structure from the classical input codes. The rows/columns that contain the support of the bit-type logical ¯ XB, ¯ZB, and check-type logical ¯ XC, ¯ZC
Figure 2 for an illustration. The number of physical or data qubits is the number of columns in (3):
FIG. 4. Combining Z stabilizers to remove the check-type qubit boxed in red. As a side effect, this nullifies the previous color group of the check-type qubits boxed in green.

FIG. 5. Example of parallel qubit reduction based on check-colorings of the classical Tanner graphs. Here we assume that χ1 = χ2 and that they are both even, and choose to combine X checks along the main diago- nal color groups {Γi,i}i=min{χ1,χ2} i=1 and Z in color groups {Γi,i+1, Γi+1,i}i=min{χ1,χ2} i=1,3,5,... . Stabilizer support from combin- ing in a particular color group is pushed into the color groups indicated by the outgoing gray arrow starting not in the cen- ter of the color group.

FIG. 6. Example HGP graph with colorings of check-type qubits denoted. Since the classical checks are 3-colorable (χ1 = χ2 = 3), the check-type qubits are split into 9 color groups. (a) The labeling of the 9 color groups Γi,j for i, j ∈{1, 2, 3}. (b) The (unoptimal) example schedule for combining stabilizers introduced in Figure 5. We combine X stabilizers on color groups Γ1,1, Γ2,2, and Γ3,3; and combine Z stabilizers on color groups Γ1,2 and Γ2,1. This schedule gets rid of 13 check-type qubits. (c) An optimal schedule to remove the most check-type qubits. By combining X stabilizers on color groups Γ1,1, Γ2,3, Γ3,1 and combining Z stabilizers on color groups Γ2,1, Γ1,2, Γ1,3, we remove 18...
